The model provides insights into the availability and status of features across Metabase instances on a daily basis. The data is based on the anonymous stats ping we receive from Metabase instances. **Key use cases include:** - Feature adoption: Understanding which features are most commonly enabled and available across instances. - Analyzing trends in feature availability over time to inform product development and marketing strategies. **Granularity:** One row per feature per instance per day ## Caveats: - This data only shows instances that opted into sending anonymous stats pings (cloud instances have opt-in by default). - We can only identify cloud instances with their `account_id`. The identifcation might not work right away due to potential delays in the ETL process, which may affect data completeness for new instances. - Feature availability was only implemented into the stats ping from Metabase version 51.0 onwards. ## Playbook: Example SQL queries demonstrating how to leverage this model. **Get the feature availability for a specific instance:** ```sql select metabase_instance_id, feature_name, is_available, is_enabled from dbt_models_product_events.daily_instance_feature_availability where metabase_instance_id = '<your_instance_id>' and date = '<specific_date>' ``` **Retrieve the features available for all paying instances:** ```sql select metabase_instance_id, feature_name, is_available, metabase_plan from dbt_models_product_events.daily_instance_feature_availability where is_paying_instance = true ```
Unique identifier for the feature availability record.
The date for which feature availability is reported.
The name of the feature being tracked.
Indicates if the feature is available for the instance (i.e. can it be enabled).
Indicates if the feature is enabled for the instance (i.e. it can be used).
Indicates if the instance is on a paid Metabase plan.
The Metabase plan associated with the instance.
The DNS name of the Metabase instance. Note: This is only available for cloud instances if we could match the instance to an account.
The anonymous ID of the Metabase instance.
The account ID associated with the instance. Note: This is only available for cloud instances if we could match the instance to an account.
The ID of the unified instance event.
The version of Metabase being used.

The model aggregates key metrics related to the usage of features across Metabase instances on a daily basis. It provides insights into the number of instances utilizing specific features, as well as their availability and enabled status. **Key use cases include:** - Use as denominator for calculating feature adoption rates or when comparing to related feature events - Evaluating feature utilization trends over time to inform product development. - Analyzing the availability and engagement of features among paying customers compared to free instances. **Granularity:** One row per feature per day ## Caveats: - The model only accounts for instances that have opted into sending anonymous stats, potentially leading to incomplete data for non-cloud instances. - Counts are based on unique (anonymous) instance identifiers - Feature availability was only implemented into the stats ping from Metabase version 51.0 onwards. ## Playbook: Example SQL queries demonstrating how to leverage this model. **Get daily feature statistics for a specific feature:** ```sql select date, feature_name, all_instances, instances_with_feature_available, feature_available_rate from dbt_models_product_events.daily_instance_feature_stats where feature_name = '<feature_name>' ```
The date for which feature statistics are reported.
The name of the feature being tracked.
The total number of unique instances that sent the stats ping.
The count of instances where the feature is available for use (i.e. can be enabled).
The count of instances where the feature is actively enabled (i.e. it can be used).
The percentage of instances where the feature is available.
The percentage of instances with the feature available where the feature is enabled (i.e. it can be used).
The count of paying instances that sent the stats ping.
The count of paying instances where the feature is available for use (i.e. can be enabled).
The count of paying instances where the feature is actively enabled (i.e. it can be used).
The percentage of paying instances where the feature is available.
The percentage of paying instances with the feature available where the feature is enabled (i.e. it can be used).

This model provides the daily metrics collected by anonymous stats pings received from Metabase instances. **Key use cases include:** - **Performance Monitoring:** Track key performance indicators (KPIs) to ensure instances are running optimally. - **Feature Usage Analysis:** Understand how different features are being utilized across instances to inform product development and enhancements. - **Trend Analysis:** Identify trends in metric values over time to detect potential issues or areas for improvement. - **Segment Analysis:** Compare metrics across different plans **Granularity:** One row per metric (per group) per instance per day. ## Caveats: - **Data Completeness:** This data only shows instances that opted into sending anonymous stats pings (cloud instances have opt-in by default). - **ETL Delays:** We can only identify cloud instances with their `account_id`. The identifcation might not work right away due to potential delays in the ETL process, which may affect data completeness for new instances. - **Metric Availability:** Only metrics tracked by Metabase version 51.0 and later are included. - **Metric groups:** Some metrics are grouped into categories. Those will have a `metric_group` value. For individual metrics, this field will be `null`. ## Playbook: Example SQL queries demonstrating how to leverage this model. **Retrieve metrics for a specific instance on a specific date:** ```sql select metabase_anonymous_instance_id, metric_name, metric_group, value, is_paying_instance, metabase_plan from dbt_models_product_events.daily_instance_metrics where metabase_anonymous_instance_id = '<your_instance_id>' and date = '2024-12-05' ``` **Analyze average metric values for all paying instances:** ```sql select metric_name, avg(value) as average_value from dbt_models_product_events.daily_instance_metrics where is_paying_instance = true group by metric_name order by average_value desc ``` **Track metric trends over the past month for a specific metric:** ```sql select date, value from dbt_models_product_events.daily_instance_metrics where metric_name = 'query_execution_time' and date >= current_date - interval '30 day' order by date ``` **Analyze a grouped metric**: ```sql select metric_group, metric_name, avg(value) as average_value from dbt_models_product_events.daily_instance_metrics where metric_group = 'query_execution' group by metric_group, metric_name order by average_value desc ```

The numerical value of the metric.

Data Complexity Score events from Metabase instances. The Data Complexity Score is computed per instance from a hierarchical formula across multiple catalogs (`library`, `universe`, `metabot`). Each catalog is composed of `<group>` rollups that aggregate `<group>.<component>` leaves into a single weighted score. **Granularity:** One row per (catalog, key) slice per instance per scoring run. A single scoring run on an instance emits multiple rows: one for the catalog `total`, one per `<group>.total` rollup, and one per `<group>.<component>` leaf. **What can this be used for:** * Tracking the complexity score distribution across instances and over time * Drilling into which components drive a customer's overall complexity * Comparing scoring formula versions when the formula changes * Identifying instances that fail to compute specific sub-scores (see `error`) **Caveats:** * `score` is null when the leaf's measurement couldn't be computed, or when an aggregate rollup includes any uncomputed leaf — check `error` (only set on the originating leaf) to see why. * `measurement` is absent on aggregate rows (`*.total` and the catalog `total`) and null on uncomputed leaves. * When comparing scores across time, filter by `formula_version` — values are not directly comparable across formula versions. * `parameters` is a free-form JSON object holding scoring inputs that are likely to evolve (tunables, embedder identity). Sorted-key serialization means it's safe to use as a SQL group-by.
Surrogate key composed of the event's root_id and root_tstamp.
Name of the event (always `data_complexity_scoring`).
Timestamp of when the event was created.
Timestamp of when the event was collected.
Which catalog of the data layer was scored — one of `library`, `universe`, `metabot`.
Dotted identifier for this slice: `total` for the catalog rollup, `<group>.total` for a group rollup, or `<group>.<component>` for a leaf. Free-form so new keys can be added without a schema bump.
Weighted score for this key. Null when the sub-score (or any sub-score it aggregates) couldn't be computed.
Raw pre-score measurement. Absent on aggregate rows; null on uncomputed leaves.
Failure message on the originating leaf when its sub-score couldn't be computed. Aggregates carry null `score` rather than propagating the error.
Scoring formula version. Bumps on breaking changes — filter on this when comparing scores across time.
JSON object with scoring inputs that are likely to evolve (tunables, embedder identity). Sorted-key serialized so it's stable for SQL group-by.

UUID identifying the scoring run that emitted this row. All (catalog, key) slices from a single scoring pass on one instance share a `batch_id`, letting you reconstruct the full set of scores published together. Generated once per scoring run on the source instance and emitted with every event in that run.

Timestamp of the earliest event in this batch (`min(created_at)` over all rows sharing this `batch_id`). Use this as the canonical "when did this scoring run happen" timestamp instead of `created_at`, which varies per row within a batch.

The model aggregates key metrics related to the usage of features across Metabase instances on a monthly basis. It provides insights into the number of instances utilizing specific features, as well as their availability and enabled status. **Key use cases include:** - Use as denominator for calculating feature adoption rates or when comparing to related feature events - Evaluating feature utilization trends over time to inform product development. - Analyzing the availability and engagement of features among paying customers compared to free instances. **Granularity**: One row per feature per month ## Caveats: - The model only accounts for instances that have opted into sending anonymous stats, potentially leading to incomplete data for non-cloud instances. - Counts are based on unique (anonymous) instance identifiers - Feature availability was only implemented into the stats ping from Metabase version 51.0 onwards. ## Playbook: Example SQL queries demonstrating how to leverage this model. **Get monthly feature statistics for a specific feature:** ```sql select month, feature_name, all_instances, instances_with_feature_available, feature_available_rate from dbt_models_product_events.monthly_instance_feature_stats where feature_name = '<feature_name>' ```
The month for which feature statistics are reported.
The name of the feature being tracked.
The total number of unique instances that sent the stats ping.
The count of instances where the feature is available for use (i.e. can be enabled).
The count of instances where the feature is actively enabled (i.e. it can be used).
The percentage of instances where the feature is available.
The percentage of instances with the feature available where the feature is enabled (i.e. it can be used).
The count of paying instances that sent the stats ping.
The count of paying instances where the feature is available for use (i.e. can be enabled).
The count of paying instances where the feature is
The percentage of paying instances where the feature is available.
The percentage of paying instances with the feature available where the feature is enabled (i.e. it can be used).

**Purpose and Utility**: The table tracks the usage of quotas for various hosting features within the platform. It is designed to answer key questions such as: - How much of a particular resource is currently being used by hosted instances? - What are the thresholds for alerting users about approaching or exceeding usage limits? - Which instances are locked due to quota overages? This data supports use cases such as monitoring resource usage, managing hosting capacities, and ensuring that instances operate within their allotted quotas. **Granularity**: One row per instance per hosting feature **Update Frequency**: Every 2 hours
Unique identifier for each record in the table. This ID is a UUID that ensures each entry is distinct.
Timestamp indicating when the quota usage record was first created.
Timestamp indicating the last time the record was updated. This can be used to track changes in quota usage over time.
Timestamp indicating when the record was marked as deleted (soft delete). Records with this field populated are no longer active but are retained for historical analysis.
Describes the specific hosting feature being tracked (e.g., clickhouse-dwh). This field helps in identifying which resource's usage is being monitored.
Indicates the type of quota applied to the hosting feature, such as number of rows for the data warehouse feature.
Represents the current usage of the hosting feature by the instance. The unit of measurement varies depending on the hosting feature and quota_type.
The usage level at which the next alert will be triggered for the hosted instance.
A boolean field that indicates whether the hosted instance is locked due to exceeding its quota.
A unique identifier for the hosted instance associated with the quota usage. This ID links the quota usage data to a specific instance.

This model generates a unified view of important IDs related to a customers. It can be used to join information from different sources to a single customer. ## Caveats - Be aware that some customers might share the same email domain or other ids (e.g. pipedrive). When joining for multiple customers, this might cause duplicates in the result set. ## Playbook examples Get all ms store tokens for a customer (works similar for other ids): ```sql select customer_id, token from dbt_models_staging.stg_customer_id_mappings cross join unnest(ms_store_tokens) as token where customer_id = <id> ``` Get all deals for a customer in Pipedrive: ```sql select customer_id, deal_id from dbt_models_staging.stg_customer_id_mappings left join <deal-model> as deal on deal.organization_id = any(pipedrive_organization_ids) where customer_id = <id> ```

An array of tokens from the Metabase Store associated with the customer.
An array of Stripe customer IDs associated with the customer.
An array of Pipedrive organization IDs associated with the customer.
An array of Pipedrive lead IDs associated with the customer.

This model contain the token usage information for LLM calls that happened in ai-service. Every LLM call in ai-service will create a new row in this table with the usage information. **What can this be used for*** This model was built to get an understanding of the LLM token usage of customers and can be used for that. Example questions it can answer: * How many tokens did customer X use in a specific time period? * Which features (using the `tag` as source) generated most usage (and costs) across customers? * What is the average response duration for LLM calls? * How many tokens does feature X (tag) use per request on average? * What is the average token consumption (+ costs) per user per time period? **Granularity:** One row per LLM call **Caveats**: * There can be multiple LLM calls within a single request (e.g. user chats with Metabot and Metabot performs multiple loops with tool calls where every LLM invocation would create a new event). However, you can aggregate those to a request level using the `request_id`. * LLM calls can be triggered by different sources (e.g. Metabot, Document generation, etc.). Use the `tag` column to identify which source / feature triggered the LLM call.
Surrogate key composed of the event's root_id and root_tstamp.
Timestamp when the event occurred.
Session identifier that can be used to group request as part of the same conversation.
The number of completion tokens.
The estimated cost of for the tokens in USD.

The hashed value of the Metabase license token for instances with a token. For Open Source instances, this will use the snowplow anonymous uuid in the form `oss_<anonymous_uuid>` to allow unique counts of instances.
The ID of the model that was used to generate the tokens.
The number of prompt tokens.
Unique ID that is assigned to each incoming request. Can be used to aggregate usage for a single request.
The total number of tokens.
The time it took to (in milliseconds) to receive an answer from the LLM.
The ID of the user that send the request.
A classifier indicating the specific purpose or functionality for which the tokens were used.
The profile/configuration variant used for the request. Allows distinguishing different configuration variants and usage patterns. Introduced in schema version 1-0-4.

The source/trigger of the request (e.g., 'metabot_agent', 'sql_generation_workflow', 'analyze_chart'). Indicates which API endpoint or workflow initiated the LLM call. Introduced in schema version 1-0-4.
